segments.text: If you dive 1,000 meters into the ocean,
you might hear a massive boom. This
happened in 1997. The noise was so loud
it was like hearing something happen in
New York from Salt Lake City. Louder
than any animal we've ever heard. And we
couldn't see anything nearby. So, what
was it? Sound is carried by vibrating
molecules bumping into one another. And
since water is more dense than air,
sound waves travel over four times
faster here. But temperature and
pressure can affect that. Up here, the
water's warm, so molecules move faster
and so does sound until it's scattered
by ocean waves. But the deeper you go,
the colder it gets. So here, molecules
move slower and so does sound. But
around here, the extreme pressure
actually tenses the molecules, letting
sound travel fast again until it's
absorbed by the seafloor. So we have
these layers, and right here in this
slow zone, certain sound waves get
trapped. They bounce between the faster
layers without ever touching the surface
or the seafloor, letting them travel for
thousands of kilometers, which is why we
often hear those mysterious noises, like
that big boom dubbed the bloop.
And they finally identified it was an
iceberg breaking off Antarctica. But
there are other weird things going on in
the deep. To find out what, subscribe.
